items.mistake: Transposing the Jacobian when applying the chain rule
items.fix: Remember J_{h∘f} = J_h · J_f with shapes matching: J_h is p×m, J_f is m×n, product is p×n.
items.why: Wrong layout swaps rows and columns and breaks the linear approximation Δ(h∘f) ≈ J_h J_f Δx.
items.example: If f: ℝ²→ℝ³ and h: ℝ³→ℝ², then J_f is 3×2 and J_h is 2×3; their product is 2×2, not 3×3.

items.mistake: Assuming Hessian symmetry without checking smoothness
items.fix: H_ij = H_ji holds when f is C²; at kinks or if mixed partials fail to commute, symmetry may break in theory.
items.why: Symmetric Hessians justify using eigendecomposition for curvature; asymmetric matrices mislead optimization analysis.
items.example: f(x,y) = |xy| has a non-classical second derivative at (0,0); do not blindly apply min/max tests there.

items.mistake: Concluding a global minimum from a positive definite Hessian at one point
items.fix: Positive definite H at x* with ∇f(x*)=0 proves a *local* minimum only; non-convex losses have many local basins.
items.why: Neural network losses are highly non-convex; local curvature does not describe the entire parameter space.
items.example: A 2-layer net may have H positive definite at weight set A but a lower loss at distant weights B.

items.mistake: Computing the full Jacobian for every training step
items.fix: Use vector-Jacobian products (VJP) or reverse-mode autograd, which compute gradients w.r.t. parameters without forming the full matrix.
items.why: Materializing J for a layer with 4096 inputs and 4096 outputs costs 16M entries per sample—wasteful when only one row (loss gradient) is needed.
items.example: `loss.backward()` in PyTorch never builds the full m×n Jacobian of each layer; it applies VJPs along the graph.
